text: laparoscopic truncal vagotomy, and gastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic truncal vagotomy followed by a gastrojejunostomy, using laparoscopic instruments to access and manipulate the stomach and jejunum.
The surgeon transects the gastrohepatic ligament, revealing the underlying right crus of the diaphragm.
The surgeon begins the dissection by transecting the gastrohepatic ligament, separating it from the surrounding structures.
After transecting the gastrohepatic ligament, the right crus of the diaphragm is exposed, revealing its muscular fibers.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the peritoneum at the inferior aspect of the right cruse, revealing underlying anatomical structures.

Clips are placed on either side of the posterior vagus nerve, securing it in position after dissection.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make incisions, known as teratomies, in preparation for the gastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon creates the gastrojejunostomy by using a 60mm blue load stapler to join the loop of jejunum to the posterior wall of the stomach.

The surgeon closes the defect created during the gastrojejunostomy procedure, ensuring the surgical site is properly sealed.
The surgeon closes the defect in two layers using two ovicral sutures, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue edges.
The surgeon completes the gastrojejunostomy by suturing in two layers using 2-0 Vicryl sutures, securing the connection between the stomach and jejunum.
